turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: And -- if he says that he is not the same person, a jury must be empanelled to try the question of identity to determine whether or not he is the same person. And if he acknowledges after being duly cautioned, the court sentences him. Now is practice then a question has been raised and continue to say have been granted.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I beg pardon?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Continue to say have been granted it so that this question can be determined.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What question of identity?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Identity.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, suppose a man -- suppose the man says, “Yes”. I am the person referred to in this information, but I was pardoned by the Governor of Pennsylvania because he found I was innocent and my -- my other conviction was voided by the courts because they found that it was -- I was unconstitutionally convicted, but I -- but I am the person was who mentioned in that information,” can he raise those questions?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: He can raise those questions. If he didn't -- well, let's go back, if he denied that he is entitled to a jury trial and the State must then put on evidence.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, no, that isn't what I'm talking about. He admits that he is the person referred to in those -- in the information. Does that end his defense or does he have other defenses such as he had received a full pardon from the Government because of his -- because of his innocence or he had had his other conviction reversed because he was unconstitutionally convicted? Can he raise those -- those issues if he admits his identity?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Yes, he could raise those issues.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Alright. Now, what law of your state gives it? Is there a statute concerning that or is that interpretation of your Supreme Court?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Then -- the Court has -- not rules specifically on that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: And we have observed it in practice.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You what -- you what?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: It had been observed in practice that you can raise any question that has to do with whether or not the conviction is proper one to sustain the application of the habitual criminal law.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, under what statute do you do that?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: We have no specific statute on that sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And how -- how frequently has that been done in practice --

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) very infrequently.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I beg pardon?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Infrequently. It's not been frequent.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do you then in your knowledge have in mind any cases where it's done?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: I have observed several in the -- in immediate court into North County where it has been raised and if -- the prisoner was permitted to show this discrepancy or whatever merit was that would show that he -- the conviction that was being set forth was not a proper one or application under habitual criminal statute.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Are there -- are those opinions reported?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: No sir, they're not reported.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: There are no opinions reported opinions (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: On that question, no sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You use to answer Mr. Chief Justice that it -- as a matter of practice, he's allowed to interpose considerations other than lack of (Inaudible) and mis-identity. I think you said --

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- practice. But I want to know you've also said there is no decision dealing with it. Is there any reference in any -- in any opinion, either by the lower court or by your Supreme Court? Is there any statement in Crabtree which reflects that practice? Is there any statement? I don't mean an adjudication but very often, judges in writing, state as the matter of fact, reference so to the fact under this State. Do you recall any such reference shorter than actual adjudication?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: No Mr. Justice Frankfurter, I do not.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now what I looked at the briefs as -- has talked to Mr. Ginsburg in the Graham case and the Graham -- Graham was -- the case was argued here, on the brief there is -- one of the (Inaudible) lawyers of this day Mr. Frank Holden and he takes it for granted. I'm not suggesting that that's controlling law but (Inaudible) shed some light in this groping darkness and in that brief that it said, “The only defense opened in West Virginia is identical,” the issue of identification. As I say that -- that goes no more than accounts a statement. It is interesting to me that none of these questions was raised in that case. Maybe there's no basis for raising any. The Graham case went entirely in challenging the method for determining identity namely, by way of information against in your State, you have to have an indictment (Inaudible) felony.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm just groping to see whether there's anything in any legal, in any weighty pronouncement by any judge that as a matter of practice, Mr. Hogan was wrong in saying the only defense is to establish identification.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Not and think of no -- not in publish to that effect.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you speak out of you own experience.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You've been -- you've been in the Justice Department of the State of West Virginia, how long Mr. Mitchell?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: This is my fifth year.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Your fifth year.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Yes, sir, I was in practice --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) you presented criminal cases?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Mostly, always.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Pardon me.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: It just went on and my record has been in a craft.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And the -- and the records come over or the proceedings come over your desk (Inaudible)

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: That's true sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But Mr. Mitchell, the one of professors of law school made to rather extensive study of this subject, did he not?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he --

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: Mr. Bryant.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- did he comment on -- on this precise question that Justice Frankfurter now you're discussing that?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: No, he did not.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: He didn't go in to that?

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: GEORGE H. MITCHELL
turns.text: It's been pointed out, petitioner Crabtree was indicted by the grand jury of Lincoln County West Virginia for the offense of forging a $35 check. He was arraigned and entered a plea of guilty and then one -- perhaps in one week later, the prosecuting attorney filed an information --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We will recess now Mr. Mitchell.
